title: Potatoes
prose: SOIL REQUIREMENTS Potatoes are generally grown on soils with less than 25% clay in the topsoil. Sandy to sandy loam soils with a reasonable amount of organic matter and with a depth of at least one metre or more, are ideal. The water holding capacity of the subsoil is important for dryland production where planting usually commences before the onset of the summer rains. Sufficient moisture should be stored in the subsoil to support the crop for approximately six to eight weeks. Soils with gravelly subsoil layers should therefore be avoided for early dryland establishment. Soils with clayey, impermeable subsoil layers near the surface should be avoided. These soils usually are poorly drained and could cause tubers to rot. SOIL ANALYSES Fields destined for potato production should preferably be identified well in advance, so that soil analyses are done at least a year before planting commences. Any adjustments required can then be done at an early stage. It is particularly important to complete Ca adjustments in good time. This method is more successful than implementing calcium adjustments during the planting season. CLIMATIC REQUIREMENTS Potatoes are a cool season crop, with an optimum air temperature of 20 °C. High air and soil temperatures retard or prevent tuber development, cause various tuber deformities and promote eelworm (nematode) development. Furthermore, high soil temperatures are responsible for a low total tuber mass per plant. Cultivars show considerable variation in sensitivity to high temperatures. Time of planting should therefore be manipulated to allow tuber initiation during relatively cool periods. In virtually all potato producing areas, November and December are unsuitable planting dates because of high air and soil temperatures. NUTRIENT AND QUALITY REQUIREMENTS With the processing industry expanding year by year, the specific requirements of the industry must be borne in mind when planning a fertilization program. A large number of cultivars are already being planted solely for the processing industry. In this industry, inner potato quality is the crucial factor, ultimately determining prices. Nitrogen (N) Nitrogen is one of the main nutrients required for growth, and deficiencies can lead to direct losses of yield. Too much N can be just as harmful, by producing excessive leaf growth at the cost of tuber development. The storage life is also reduced, together with the relative density (specific gravity), whilst hollow heart can be more prominent in a cultivar such as BP1. As a rule, the N-content of the soil is not determined by soil analysis. It is linked to microbiological activity in the soil, and this complicates the interpretation of results. N-guidelines for different cultivation methods are provided in Tables 5.6.2.1 to 5.6.2.3. Table 5.6.2.1. Guideline for N-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) at different yield potentials for dryland cultivation on soils with different clay contents * Consult the processing industry for relative specifications and cultivars. These guidelines have to be adapted in accordance with rainfall, history of the field, soil type, plant density and planting date. Nitrogen is easily leached from the soil, and irrigation therefore has to be applied judiciously. For this reason it is advisable to apply a portion of the total amount of nitrogen as a topdressing, especially on light textured soils (Table 5.6.2.3). Table 5.6.2.2. N-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) at different yield potentials for cultivation under irrigation on soils of different texture classes Table 5.6.2.3. Proposed times for N-application on soils for different texture classes, expressed as a percentage of total N-fertilization Phosphorus (P) Phosphorus is an important constituent of all plant cells. Sufficient quantities stimulate early root development and increase the plant's water consumption efficiency. P-deficiencies result in poor keeping quality of the tubers. P-fertilizer recommendations are based on soil analyses. The P-content of soil can be determined in various ways. Guidelines for P-fertilization appear in Table 5.6.2.4. Phosphorus is not easily leached from the soil and can therefore be applied one-off at planting. Table 5.6.2.4. Guidelines for P-fertilization according to different soil analysis methods Note: The relationship between P-analyses methods in the table is based on averages and may vary greatly depending on pH and other factors (FSSA Ed.). Potassium (K) Potassium promotes the production of starch and is also important for certain reactions in the photosynthesis process. Over-abundance of potassium in the soil can result in poor tuber quality. Deficiencies may also be detrimental to quality (dry chip colour and relative density). Potassium requirements should not be judged solely on the concentration (mg kg -1 ) according to the soil analysis. The ratio of potassium to the cation exchange capacity (CEC) of the soil is also important. Producers must therefore insist that the soil CEC is determined during analysis. If the CEC is not determined but the soil acidity has been estimated (titratable hydrogen or H + expressed as cmol c kg -1 (me%)) the soil CEC can be calculated as indicated in Step 1. The sum total of the basic cations (Na, K, Ca, Mg) and the titratable hydrogen then represents the CEC. Example: Assume the following example of a soil analysis: The following steps are now taken to determine the quantity of potassium as a percentage of the CEC: Step 1: If the concentration of potassium, magnesium, calcium and sodium in the soil analysis was indicated as mg kg -1 , this should be converted to me (milli-equivalents) per 100 g of soil (also expressed as cmol c kg -1 ). This is done as follows: The hydrogen concentration is added to the total to arrive at the CEC: 5.31 + 0.18 = 5.49 Therefore, the CEC of the soil is 5.49 cmol c kg -1 Step 2: The quantity of potassium is then calculated as a percentage of the CEC, for example: If the CEC is not known and cannot be calculated using the above method, the potassium ratio can be calculated as a percentage of the total quantity of cations as obtained in Step 1, namely: The potassium requirement of the soil can now be determined in accordance with Table 5.6.2.5 or 5.6.2.6. Calcium (Ca) and magnesium (Mg) fertilization Calcium and magnesium deficiencies can be calculated in the same way as for potassium. The following norms indicate the desired percentage of saturation of the two cations: Magnesium: 15 to 20% of the CEC Calcium: 60 to 70% of the CEC Table 5.6.2.5. Guideline for K-fertilization according to soil analysis on soils with a CEC > 6 Table 5.6.2.6. Guideline for K-fertilization according to soil analysis on soils with a CEC < 6 On soils with a CEC > 6, Table 5.6.2.7 is used for calcium recommendations (as gypsum), and Table 5.6.2.8 is used for soils with a CEC < 6. Recommendations for magnesium fertilization are made with reference to Table 5.6.2.9. The recommendations in Table 5.6.2.9 are valid for magnesium oxide. If other products are used, such as magnesium sulphate, magnesium nitrate or magnesite, the solubility of these products should be taken into account. Table 5.6.2.7. Guideline for Ca- (as gypsum) fertilization according to soil analysis on soils with a CEC > 6 Table 5.6.2.8. Guideline for Ca- (as gypsum) fertilization according to soil analysis on soils with a CEC < 6 Table 5.6.2.9. Guideline for Mg-fertilization according to soil analyses Micro-elements It is recommended that the hot water extraction method be used for topsoil analysis. Since problems are encountered with soil analysis, it is recommended that the international norms of leaf analysis, which are available in textbooks, be used to determine whether micro-nutrient supplementation should be applied. Leaf samples should always be taken in accordance with the prescribed growth stage, the part of the plant and the position on the plant, otherwise incorrect deductions may be made. Micro-element recommendations should always be made by a competent adviser. Organic fertilizers Potatoes react favourably to applied organic fertilizers such as fully composted kraal manure and compost. Fresh, undecomposed material can, however, increase the occurrence of scab and may also lead to a temporary nitrogen negative period. When in doubt about the quality of available manures it would be preferable to apply the manure to a preceding crop. Organic fertilizer can therefore substitute part of the inorganic fertilizer in the fertilizer programme. Placement The potato plant has a poorly developed root system and consequently a relatively poor utilisation of applied fertilizers. Optimum yields therefore require more fertilizer than is actually taken up by the plant and must be applied near and below the plant. The broadcasting of fertilizer before drawing furrows is not effective. Broadcasting after the drawing of furrows is more effective, as some of the fertilizer will be concentrated near the tubers. This method is recommended for the application of superphosphate before planting on low P soils or for the application of gypsum as a source of calcium. When fertilizer is broadcast, ensure that it does not come into contact with the tubers. Any such contact can damage tubers and in some cases even cause the potato eyes to die back. The most effective method of application, however, remains placement in the furrow below and slightly away from the tubers. ACKNOWLEDGEMENT The input of Mr. Div Bosman in revising this chapter is gratefully acknowledged.
